qa_descs.phrase_lookup: The slide's main intention is to compare the performance of different LLM-based rule repositories in terms of their ability to induce Stage-2 prompt generation in a poisoning attack.
qa_descs.definition: This slide is mainly trying to explain the concept of Farkas Dual Rays and their role in improving LLM-based optimization model repair.
qa_descs.purpose_why: The slide is trying to convey the motivation to improve the performance of LLM-based rule repositories by addressing the limitations of existing methods.
qa_descs.mechanism_how: The Farkas Dual Rays Do Not Improve LLM-Based Optimization Model Repair method uses neural natural language optimization problems to identify and repair inconsistencies in large language models. It compares the original Farkas dual rays equations with the actual LLM model output and identifies inconsistencies. It then uses a neural network to repair the model by adjusting its weights to minimize the difference between the two.
qa_descs.visual_identification: FARKAS DUAL RAYS DO NOT IMPROVE LLM-BASED OPTIMIZATION MODEL REPAIR

title_chunks.text: Sequence to Sequence Generation with Transformer Based Models

body_chunks.text: Sequence to Sequence Generation with Transformer Based Models
• Input: Sequence x1, …, xT • Output: Sequence y1, …, yT’

• Transformer is an encoder-decoder architecture: • An encoder (neural network) analyzes the input and builds an intermediate representation (a.k.a. context vector) of it; • A decoder (neural network) analyzes the intermediate representation and creates an output.

body_chunks.text: Desiderata of pos(.) : 1. It should output a unique encoding for each time-step
(word’s position in a sentence) 2. Distance between any two time-steps should be
consistent across sentences with different lengths. 3. Our model should generalize to longer sentences
without any efforts. Its values should be bounded. 4. It must be deterministic.
Source: “Attention is all you need,” NeurIPS, 2017.

qa_descs.phrase_lookup: The slide's main intention is to explain how positional encoding works in the context of neural networks. It explains that positional encoding is used to map each word in a sentence to a specific position in a d-dimensional vector, and that this allows the model to understand the relative positions of words in the sentence. The slide also mentions that this encoding is used for time-step encoding and distance calculation between sentences.
qa_descs.purpose_why: The slide is trying to convey the motivation to use positional encoding in neural networks, which is a technique to encode the position of words in a sentence.
qa_descs.mechanism_how: The slide explains how positional encoding works in a neural network. It shows how the network processes each word in a sentence and encodes its position in a vector. The vector is then used to determine the distance between words and to generate a unique encoding for each time step in the sentence.

It should output a unique encoding for each time-step (word's position in a sentence) 2. Distance between any two time-steps should be consistent across sentences with different lengths. 3. Our model should generalize to longer sentences without any efforts. Its values should be bounded. 4. It must be deterministic. Source: "Attention is all you need," NeurIPS, 2017.

qa_descs.phrase_lookup: The slide's main intention is to explain how positional encoding works in neural networks. It introduces the concept of positional encoding and how it is used to encode the position of each element in a vector. The slide also explains how this encoding is used in the self-attention mechanism and how it helps in learning relationships between different elements in the input data.
qa_descs.purpose_why: This slide is trying to convey the motivation behind positional encoding in neural networks.
qa_descs.mechanism_how: The slide explains how positional encoding works in a neural network. It shows how the network learns to associate each word in a sentence with its position in the sentence. This is done by using a special kind of encoding called self-attention, which allows the network to understand the relationships between words in the sentence. The network then uses this information to generate a more accurate and detailed representation of the sentence.

qa_descs.purpose_why: The Transformer Decoder Block (3) is designed to decode the output of the transformer encoder, which is a key component in transformer models for natural language processing tasks. The slide explains that the decoder block uses masked self-attention to interact with past inputs, which helps the model to generate meaningful and contextually relevant outputs. The motivation behind this design is to improve the model's ability to understand and generate natural
qa_descs.mechanism_how: The Transformer Decoder Block (3) is a neural network architecture used for text generation. It consists of a transformer encoder and a decoder, with the decoder taking the output of the encoder as input and generating the next word in the sequence. The decoder uses a masked self-attention mechanism to interact with past inputs and generate the next word in the sequence.
qa_descs.visual_identification: Transformer Decoder Block (3), Masked Self-attention
qa_descs.visual_alignment: The Transformer Decoder Block (3) shows the architecture of the Transformer decoder block, which is the part of the Transformer model that generates the final output sequence. The visual representation of the decoder block shows the different layers and components involved in the decoding process, such as the Transformer encoder, the masked self-attention mechanism, and the layer normalization. The masked self-attention mechanism is highlighted as a key component

body_chunks.text: n Open-web datasets used to train open text-to-image models
l To be specific, the captions in the LAION dataset, used to train vision-
language models, come from alt HTML tags (Alttext) and often describes only a narrow aspect of the image, neglecting significant visual details.
l For example, an image of a person can have as Alttext the name of the
person and the name of the photographer, but not a description of their appearance, their clothes, their position, or the background.
Source: “A Picture is Worth a Thousand Words: Principled Recaptioning Improves Image Generation,” arXiv, 2023.

body_chunks.text: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools [NeurIPS 2023]

Key steps: Given an input text x, we first sample a position i and corresponding API call candidates c1i , c2i , . . . , cki. We then execute these API calls and filter out all calls which do not reduce the loss Li over the next tokens.
All remaining API calls are interleaved with the original text, resulting in a new text x∗.

body_chunks.text: Reflexion uses verbal reinforcement to help agents learn from prior failings, ie, converting binary (eg, success/fail) or scalar feedback from the environment into verbal feedback in the form of a textual summary, which is then added as additional context for the MLLM agent in the next episode.

Reflexion utilizes three distinct models: • Actor: generating text and actions • Evaluator: scoring the outputs produced by the Actor • Self-Reflection model: generating verbal reinforcement cues to assist the Actor in self-improvement.
79 Source: “Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning,” NeurIPS, 2023.

body_chunks.text: Initialization The Actor produces a trajectory 𝜏" by interacting with the environment. The Evaluator then produces a score 𝑟" which is computed as 𝜏# = 𝑀$(𝜏"), where 𝜏# is only a scalar reward for trial 𝑡.
The Self-Reflection model analyzes the set of {𝜏", 𝑟"} to produce a summary 𝑠𝑟" which is stored in the memory 𝑚𝑒𝑚, where 𝑠𝑟# is a verbal experience feedback for trial 𝑡. Repeat
The Actor, Evaluator, and Self-Reflection model work together through trials in a loop until the Evaluator deems 𝑠𝑟# to be correct. Note that after each trial 𝑡, 𝑠𝑟#, is appended 𝑚𝑒𝑚. 80

body_chunks.text: Voyager [TMLR 2024] A procedural memory of skills
Objective: • Leveraging MLLMs (i.e., OpenAI’s APIs) for improved game mechanics understanding
Innovative Features: Introduction of automatic curriculum and skill library Automatic curriculum proposes increasingly complex tasks Skill library serves as a learning and evolution basis
Iterative prompting mechanism refines the program and incorporates it into the skill library as a new skill

title_chunks.text: The system prompt for automatic curriculum (1/3)

body_chunks.text: The system prompt for automatic curriculum (1/3)
(The list of question-answer pairs represents the additional context.)

ocr_chunks.text: You are a helpful assistant that tells me the next immediate task to do in Minecraft. My ultimate goal is to discover as many diverse things as possible, accomplish as many diverse tasks as possible and become the best Minecraft player in the world.
I will give you the following information: Question 1: Answer: Question 2:. Answer: Question 3: Answer:. Biome:. Time: Jearby blocks:. Other blocks that are recently seen:.
Nearby entities (nearest to farthest):.
Hunger: Higher than 15 means I'm not hungry. Position:. Equipment: If I have better armor in my inventory, you should ask me to equip it.

Chests: You can ask me to deposit or take items from these chests. There also might be some Completed tasks so far:. Inventory (xx/36):. unknown chest, you should ask me to open and check items inside the unknown chest.
1) You should act as a mentor and guide me to the next task based on my current learning Failed tasks that are too hard:. You must follow the following criteria:

2) Please be very specific about what resources I need to collect, what I need to craft, or 3) The next task should follow a concise format, such as "Mine [quantity] [block]", "Craft [progress. what mobs I need to kill.

4) The next task should not be too hard since I may not have the necessary resources or have [food]", "Equip [item]" etc. It should be a single phrase. Do not propose multiple tasks quantity] [item]", "Smelt [quantity] [item]", "Kill [quantity] [mob]", "Cook [quantity] at the same time. Do not mention anything else.

5) The next task should be novel and interesting. I should look for rare resources, upgrade my equipment and tools using better materials, and discover new things. I should not be doing the same thing over and over again. learned enough skills to complete it yet.
6) I may sometimes need to repeat some tasks if I need to collect more resources to complete more difficu tasks. Only repeat tasks necessary

qa_descs.mechanism_how:
The AI Weekly method involves using EEG (Electroencephalography) to analyze the brain's electrical activity during taste stimulation. The method involves three main steps: 

1. Taste stimulation: The subject is asked to taste a specific sample of food or drink.
2. Taste samples: The subject is asked to taste different samples of the same food or drink.
3. EEG recording: The subject

body_chunks.text: Two types of knowledge: implicit and explicit Implicit knowledge is typically what large-scale language models encapsulate after being trained on vast amounts of text data.

Explicit knowledge is structured and can be directly queried, such as the information found in knowledge bases or databases, which was traditionally used to enhance AI reasoning capabilities by referencing verifiable external resources.

title_chunks.text: An alternative solution (by Meta, 2024): Mixture-of-Transformers Architecture (MoT)

body_chunks.text: An alternative solution (by Meta, 2024): Mixture-of-Transformers Architecture (MoT)

• Modality-Specific Parameter Decoupling • Each modality employs a separate set of non-embedding transformer parameters – including the feedforward network, attention matrices and layer normalization. During training, each modality can be supervised using modality-specific losses.

title_chunks.text: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?

body_chunks.text: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?

• The scale of a model is one of the most important axes for better model quality. Given a fixed computing budget, training a larger model for fewer steps is better than training a smaller model for more steps.

• Mixture of Experts enable models to be pretrained with far less compute, which means you can dramatically scale up the model or dataset size with the same compute budget as a dense model. In particular, a MoE model should achieve the same quality as its dense counterpart much faster during pretraining.

chunks.text: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?
What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?
• Background

• The scale of a model is one of the most important axes for better model quality. Given a fixed computing budget, training a larger model for fewer steps is better than training a smaller model for more steps.

• Mixture of Experts enable models to be pretrained with far less compute, which means you can dramatically scale up the model or dataset size with the same compute budget as a dense model. In particular, a MoE model should achieve the same quality as its dense counterpart much faster during pretraining.
Source: https://huggingface.co/blog/moe

title_chunks.text: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?

body_chunks.text: What is a Mixture of Experts (MoE)?
• A MoE consists of two main elements:
• Sparse MoE layers are used instead of dense feed-forward network (FFN) layers. MoE layers have a certain number of “experts” (e.g. 8), where each expert is a neural network.
• A gate network or router, that determines which tokens are sent to which expert.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia A trainera is a traditional boat of the Cantabrian Sea coast at the southern end of the Bay of Biscay, propelled by oars, and formerly sailing. It is a boat of fine lines with raised prow and rounded stern, to resist the waves of the Cantabrian sea. 12 metres

Traineras were originally used by fishermen to bring in the day's catch of anchovies and sardines from sea to market, usually competing to sell their caught fish before others came in. Today, this historical tradition has become a major sport of coastal poat racing. trainera Illustration of a rowing fishing

qa_descs.mechanism_how: The slide shows a method called "REVIVE" that uses neural networks to understand visual representations of objects. The method works by first identifying the objects in an image, then using a sliding window to analyze each object region. The knowledge of the object type is then used to predict the answer to a question about the object. The method is shown to work on a specific example of a couch.

body_chunks.text: mRAG aims to provide MLLMs with more comprehensive, accurate and up-to-date world knowledge from external source. Two categories of mRAG based on the retrieval method
(1) One category employs visual encoding model for direct image representation, and then retrieves the knowledge from knowledge base with the highest feature similarity.
(2) The other category first transforms the input image into textual representation utilizing off-the-shelf tools and then performs text retrieval.

body_chunks.text: • Heuristic mRAG methods typically predefined fixed retrieval processes, suffering from challenges:
(1) Non-adaptive Retrieval Queries Referring to the fixed retrieval processes and query structures of heuristic mRAG methods (2) Overloaded Retrieval Queries
Referring to heuristic mRAG methods merely format a single query by concatenating textual descriptions of objects in images with input questions

body_chunks.text: • Dynamic questions are defined as questions that require the model to flexibly provide knowledge retrieval solutions, where the query, tool, and time of retrievals are all variable.

• These questions cannot be solved by a predefined retrieval process. • Example types of dynamic questions: • (1) Questions with rapidly changing answers • (2) Questions requiring multi-modal knowledge • (3) Multi-hop questions

body_chunks.text: Since the context knowledge of such question updates frequently, the retrieved content may be mixed with outdated and newer knowledge that is difficult to distinguish. This requires mARG methods to flexibly plan additional retrievals based on feedback from current retrieved content for further comprehension, rather than merely a one-time retrieval.

reference.title: "Benchmarking Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation with Dynamic VQA Dataset and Self-adaptive Planning Agent

body_chunks.text: OmniSearch: A self-adaptive planning agent for multimodal retrieval The underlying idea is to emulate the human behavior in question solution which dynamically decomposes complex multimodal questions into sub- question chains with retrieval action.

At each step, OmniSearch flexibly adjusts the next action according to question-solving state and retrieved content, with diverse purposes such as deepening comprehension of retrieved content, modifying retrieval method for current sub-question, proposing the next sub-question, etc.
Source: “Benchmarking Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation with Dynamic VQA Dataset and Self-adaptive Planning Agent,” ICLR, 2025.

qa_descs.mechanism_how: The Dyn-VQA dataset is a knowledge-seeking VQA dataset that allows users to ask questions about various topics and receive answers. The dataset is divided into three main parts: Knowledge Type, Answer Change Frequency, Reasoning Step, External Visual-Seek, Human Annotation, and Length of Question and Answer (Lena). The dataset is used to train and evaluate machine learning models that can answer questions about knowledge

body_chunks.text: (1) If it didn't rain, Amy visited Bobby today.
(2) Amy visited Bobby or Carson today, but not both.
Can we answer the question “Did it rain today?”
• Amy did not visit Bobby today. • It rained today.

chunks.text: An example
An example
Consider the following sentences
(1) If it didn't rain, Amy visited Bobby today.
(2) Amy visited Bobby or Carson today, but not both.
(3) Amy visited Carson today.
Can we answer the question “Did it rain today?”
• Amy did not visit Bobby today. • It rained today.

175.title: An example
175.body: Consider the following sentences (1) If it did not't rain, Amy visited Bobby today. (2) Amy visited Bobby or Carson today, but not both. (3) Amy visited Carson today. Can we answer the question "Did it rain today?" Amy did not visit Bobby today. It rained today.

The answer is that Amy did not visit Bobby today, which is a short phrase that captures the main intention of the slide.

title_chunks.text: However, when the antecedent is false, the implication is always true, regardless of the consequent. Logically, we cannot learn anything from an implication (P Q) if the antecedent (P) is false. Looking at our example, if it is not raining, the implication does not say anything about whether I'm indoors or not. I could be an indoors type and never walk outside, even when it is not raining, or I could be an outdoors type and be outside all the time when it is not raining. When the antecedent is false, we say that the implication is trivially true.

body_chunks.text: Let’s look at how knowledge and logic can be represented as code.

from logic import * # Create new classes, each having a name, or a symbol, representing each proposition. rain = Symbol("rain") # It is raining. bobby = Symbol(”bobby") # Amy visited Bobby carson = Symbol(”carson") # Amy visited Carson

# Save sentences into the KB knowledge = And( # Starting from the "And" logical connective, because each proposition represents knowledge that we know to be true. Implication(Not(rain), bobby), # ¬(It is raining) → (Amy visited Bobby) Or(bobby, carson), # (Amy visited Bobby) ∨ (Amy visited Carson).

Not(And(bobby, carson)), # ¬(Amy visited Bobby ∧ Amy visited Carson) ie Amy did not visit both Bobby and Carson. carson # Amy visited Carson. Note that while previous propositions contained multiple symbols with connectors, this is a proposition consisting of one symbol.
This means that we take as a fact that, in this KB, Amy visited Carson.)

chunks.text: A Python Code Example
A Python Code Example
Let’s look at how knowledge and logic can be represented as code.

from logic import * # Create new classes, each having a name, or a symbol, representing each proposition. rain = Symbol("rain") # It is raining. bobby = Symbol(”bobby") # Amy visited Bobby carson = Symbol(”carson") # Amy visited Carson

# Save sentences into the KB knowledge = And( # Starting from the "And" logical connective, because each proposition represents knowledge that we know to be true. Implication(Not(rain), bobby), # ¬(It is raining) → (Amy visited Bobby) Or(bobby, carson), # (Amy visited Bobby) ∨ (Amy visited Carson).

Not(And(bobby, carson)), # ¬(Amy visited Bobby ∧ Amy visited Carson) ie Amy did not visit both Bobby and Carson. carson # Amy visited Carson. Note that while previous propositions contained multiple symbols with connectors, this is a proposition consisting of one symbol.
This means that we take as a fact that, in this KB, Amy visited Carson.)

204.title: A Python Code Example
204.body: Let's look at how knowledge and logic can be represented as code. from logic import * # Create new classes, each having a name, or a symbol, representing each proposition. rain=Symbol("rain") # It is raining. bobby=Symbol("bobby") # Amy visited Bobby carson=Symbol("carson") # Amy visited Carson # Save sentences into the KB knowledge=And(# Starting from the "And" logical connective, because each proposition represents knowledge that we know to be true. Implication(Not(rain), bobby), # (It is raining) (Amy visited Bobby) Or(bobby, carson), # (Amy visited Bobby) (Amy visited Carson). Not(And(bobby, carson)), # (Amy visited Bobby Amy visited Carson) i.e. Amy did not visit both Bobby and Carson. carson # Amy visited Carson. Note that while previous propositions contained multiple symbols with connectors, this is a proposition consisting of one symbol. This means that we take as a fact that, in this KB, Amy visited Carson.)

body_chunks.text: Another type of puzzle that can be solved using propositional logic is a Mastermind game. In this game, player one arranges colors in a certain order, and then player two has to guess this order.
Each turn, player two makes a guess, and player one gives back a number, indicating how many colors player two got right. Let’s simulate a game with four colors.
